subprd.purpose: Create a new task with title and optional description, assign unique ID, set status to pending and timestamp.
subprd.description: Reads next_id from global store, creates a new task dict with id, title, description, status='pending', created_at=current time. Appends to tasks list. Increments next_id. Returns success response with task_id.

inputs.name: task_data
inputs.description: Dictionary with title and optional description.

outputs.name: result
outputs.description: Dictionary with success, message, and data containing task_id.

in_scope: Creating a task with title and description

constraints.description: Task ID must be unique and auto-incremented.

acceptance_criteria.description: Task created with correct fields and ID.

children.stop_reason: atomic_operation: read-modify-write on two data sources (tasks and next_id)
children.code:
def CreateTask(task_data: dict) -> dict:
    global tasks, next_id
    
    # Read next_id and tasks
    current_id = next_id['value']
    
    # Create new task
    new_task = {
        'id': current_id,
        'title': task_data['title'],
        'description': task_data.get('description', ''),
        'status': 'pending',
        'created_at': datetime.datetime.now().isoformat()
    }
    
    # Append to tasks
    tasks.append(new_task)
    
    # Increment next_id
    next_id['value'] = current_id + 1
    
    return {
        'success': True,
        'message': 'Task created successfully',
        'data': {'task_id': current_id}
    }

decomposition_rationale: The parent Test_prd receives a JSON input with a command and task_data. It acts as a coordinator: based on the command, it calls one of four children (CreateTask, ListTasks, CompleteTask, DeleteTask). Each child handles a specific CRUD operation. The children share the global data stores tasks and next_id. CreateTask uses both tasks and next_id; ListTasks only reads tasks; CompleteTask and DeleteTask read and write tasks. The parent parses the input, validates the command, extracts parameters, calls the appropriate child, and returns the result. This decomposition covers all functional requirements (FR-001 to FR-004) and respects the constraints (in-memory storage, single-user, CLI).

subprd.description:
INPUT FORMAT:
  Format: json
  Description: Input is a JSON object with command and task_data fields.
  Schema:
    command: string (create, list, complete, delete)
    task_data: optional dict with title, description, task_id, status_filter
  Examples:
    {"command": "create", "task_data": {"title": "Buy groceries", "description": "Milk, eggs, bread"}}
    {"command": "list", "task_data": {"status_filter": "all"}}

OUTPUT FORMAT:
  Format: json
  Description: Output is a JSON object with success status, message, and optional data.
  Schema:
    success: boolean
    message: string
    data: optional dict (e.g., task_id, tasks list)
  Examples:
    {"success": true, "message": "Task created with ID 1", "data": {"task_id": 1}}
    {"success": true, "message": "Found 1 task", "data": {"tasks": []}}

Functional Requirements:
[FR-001] Task Creation: Users can create a new task with a title and optional description. Each task gets a unique auto-incremented ID, status set to 'pending', and a creation timestamp.
[FR-002] Task Listing: Users can list all tasks, optionally filtered by status (pending/completed/all). Output shows task ID, title, status, and creation time.
[FR-003] Task Completion: Users can mark a task as completed by its ID. Must validate that the task exists and return success/error message.
[FR-004] Task Deletion: Users can delete a task by its ID. Must validate that the task exists and return success/error message.

constraints.description: Storage: memory - In-memory storage, no database persistence.
